questions.specification:
A student compares the thermal conductivity of two rods made from different materials: copper (Rod A) and steel (Rod B). Each rod has the same length, diameter, and is heated at one end with an identical electric heater. The student places thermocouples at two fixed positions on each rod and measures the temperature at those points after 300 seconds. The results are shown in the table below.

| Rod | $x_{1}(\mathrm{~cm})$ | $T_{1}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | $x_{2}(\mathrm{~cm})$ | $T_{2}\left({ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A (Copper) | 2.0 | 85.0 | 8.0 | 75.0 |
| B (Steel) | 2.0 | 75.0 | 8.0 | 35.0 |

parts.content: Explain how the temperature gradient in each rod is related to its thermal conductivity.
parts.markscheme:
- Thermal conductivity is inversely proportional to temperature gradient: $k \propto \frac{1}{\Delta T / \Delta x}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shallower gradient implies higher conductivity if input energy is constant. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
steps.content: This question explores thermal conduction, which is covered in **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. To understand the relationship, we look at how efficiently a material transfers heat relative to the temperature difference across it.

steps.title: Define Temperature Gradient
steps.content:
The temperature gradient is the change in temperature per unit length along the rod. It is calculated as:

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$$

where $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference and $\Delta x$ is the distance between the two thermocouples.

steps.title: Establish the Proportionality
steps.content:
Thermal conductivity ($k$) is a measure of how easily heat flows through a material. For a given rate of heat transfer ($P$) and cross-sectional area ($A$), the thermal conductivity is **inversely proportional** to the temperature gradient:

$$k \propto \frac{1}{\Delta T / \Delta x}$$

This means that for the same energy input, a better conductor will maintain a more uniform temperature along its length.

steps.title: Analyze the Physical Meaning
steps.content:
Since both rods are heated by an identical heater (constant energy input) and have the same dimensions, we can conclude:

1. A **shallower (smaller) gradient** indicates a **higher thermal conductivity**. This is seen in Rod A (Copper), where the temperature only drops by $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ over $6\text{ cm}$.
2. A **steeper (larger) gradient** indicates a **lower thermal conductivity**. This is seen in Rod B (Steel), where the temperature drops by $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ over the same distance.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: To earn full marks, you must state that thermal conductivity is inversely proportional to the temperature gradient ($k \propto \frac{1}{\text{gradient}}$). Then, explain that a shallower gradient implies higher conductivity because the material transfers heat more efficiently with less of a "temperature drop."

steps.title: Define Temperature Gradient
steps.content:
To understand how heat moves through the rods, we first look at the **temperature gradient**. This is the change in temperature per unit length along the rod. In this experiment, both rods are measured over the same distance interval:

$$\Delta x = x_2 - x_1 = 8.0\text{ cm} - 2.0\text{ cm} = 6.0\text{ cm}$$

This concept is part of **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**, where we study how energy moves through different materials.

steps.title: Calculate Temperature Drops
steps.content:
Now, let's calculate the temperature drop ($\Delta T$) for each rod over that 6.0 cm distance using the data from the table:

- **Rod A (Copper):**
$$\Delta T_A = 85.0^{\circ}\text{C} - 75.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 10.0^{\circ}\text{C}$$

- **Rod B (Steel):**
$$\Delta T_B = 75.0^{\circ}\text{C} - 35.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 40.0^{\circ}\text{C}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 85 - 75 for Rod A and 75 - 35 for Rod B.

desmos.instructions: Perform the subtractions in separate lines.

steps.title: Compare the Gradients
steps.content:
We can see that for the same distance (6.0 cm), Rod A has a much smaller temperature drop ($10.0^{\circ}\text{C}$) compared to Rod B ($40.0^{\circ}\text{C}$).

This means Rod A has a **shallower temperature gradient** than Rod B. Quantitatively:

$$\text{Gradient}_A = \frac{10.0^{\circ}\text{C}}{6.0\text{ cm}} \approx 1.67^{\circ}\text{C/cm}$$
$$\text{Gradient}_B = \frac{40.0^{\circ}\text{C}}{6.0\text{ cm}} \approx 6.67^{\circ}\text{C/cm}$$

steps.title: Apply Energy Transfer Concept
steps.content:
Thermal conductivity ($k$) represents the "ease" with which heat flows through a material. If a material is a good conductor, it can transfer a large amount of energy without requiring a large temperature difference between two points. 

Therefore, for a constant energy input, a **shallower temperature gradient** (smaller $\frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$) indicates a **higher thermal conductivity**.

Since Rod A maintains a more uniform temperature (smaller gradient) while transferring the same energy, it has a higher thermal conductivity than Rod B.

We can see that the magnitude of the gradient for Copper ($1.67^{\circ}\text{C/cm}$) is much **smaller** than that for Steel ($6.67^{\circ}\text{C/cm}$).

This indicates that **Copper has a higher thermal conductivity** than Steel. steps.title: Establish the proportionality
steps.content:
In **Topic B.1 (Thermal energy transfers)**, we learn that materials with high thermal conductivity ($k$) are better at transferring energy. 

If two rods have the same cross-sectional area and receive the same energy input, the thermal conductivity is inversely proportional to the magnitude of the temperature gradient:

$$k \propto \frac{1}{\left| \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x} \right|}$$

This means a material that is a 'good' conductor will show a very small (shallow) change in temperature over a distance, while a 'poor' conductor will show a large (steep) change.

steps.title: Analyze Copper (Rod A)
steps.content:
From our calculation, Copper has a much smaller gradient magnitude ($1.67$ vs $6.67$). 

Because the gradient is shallower, it indicates that Copper is more efficient at distributing thermal energy along its length. Therefore, Copper has a **higher thermal conductivity**.

steps.title: Analyze Steel (Rod B)
steps.content:
Steel has a much steeper gradient magnitude ($6.67$). 

This steep drop in temperature over the same distance shows that Steel is less effective at conducting heat compared to Copper. Therefore, Steel has a **lower thermal conductivity**.

parts.content: Suggest two improvements to this experiment that would improve the reliability of the comparison.
parts.markscheme:
- Insulate rods to reduce heat loss to surroundings. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use more thermocouple positions along the rod or record temperature over time to ensure steady-state. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Minimize heat loss
steps.content:
In an ideal thermal conduction model, all thermal energy flows directly from the hot end to the cold end. However, in this setup, energy is lost from the surface of the rods to the surroundings through convection and radiation. 

To improve reliability and ensure that the temperature drop is due to the material's resistance to conduction rather than environmental loss, we should **insulate the rods** with a material like foam or wool.

steps.title: Ensure steady-state conditions
steps.content:
The student recorded temperatures after a fixed time of 300 seconds. For a reliable comparison of thermal conductivity, the system must reach a **steady state**, where the temperature at any given point no longer changes with time. 

Because copper and steel have different thermal properties (like specific heat capacity), they may take different amounts of time to reach this state. A better procedure would be to **record the temperature over time** and only take final readings once the temperatures have stabilized.

highlights.text: measures the temperature at those points after 300 seconds

steps.title: Increase data density
steps.content:
Using only two thermocouples provides a single temperature gradient calculation. To verify that the temperature decrease is linear and to reduce the impact of random errors or local fluctuations, the student should **use more thermocouple positions** along the length of each rod. 

This allows for a line of best fit on a temperature-distance graph, providing a more accurate measure of the thermal gradient.

highlights.text: places thermocouples at two fixed positions

steps.title: Analyze the experimental limitations
steps.content:
To improve the reliability of an experiment comparing thermal conductivity, we need to ensure that the variables we are measuring are as accurate as possible and that the conditions match the theoretical model. 

In this experiment, the student measures the temperatures at two points after a fixed time. This is related to **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**. Reliability can be improved by reducing energy losses and ensuring the system has reached a stable condition.

highlights.text: measures the temperature at those points after 300 seconds

steps.title: Insulate the rods
steps.content:
One major source of error in thermal experiments is heat loss to the surroundings. 

By **insulating the rods** (also called lagging), we ensure that the thermal energy from the electric heater travels primarily *through* the rod via conduction rather than being lost to the air via convection or radiation. This makes the temperature gradient more uniform and the comparison between the copper and steel rods more valid. 

This would earn you **1 mark**.

steps.title: Increase data points
steps.content:
The current method only uses two thermocouples. If one sensor is slightly miscalibrated or poorly positioned, it creates a large error in the calculated temperature difference.

A common **General Reliability Improvement** is to **use more thermocouple positions** along the rod. By plotting a graph of temperature versus distance for many points, you can find a line of best fit. This reduces the impact of random errors in any single measurement. 

Alternatively, you could record the temperature over a longer period to ensure the rod has reached a **steady-state** (where temperatures no longer change with time). This would earn you the second **1 mark**.

steps.title: Identify the key assumption
steps.content:
In thermal conductivity experiments, we are interested in how heat moves through a material once the temperature distribution has stabilized. By taking readings at a specific time, the student is assuming that the system has reached **steady-state heat flow**.

This means we assume that 300 seconds is a long enough duration for the temperature at every point along the rods to stop changing with time.

highlights.text: measures the temperature at those points after 300 seconds

steps.title: Explain the steady-state concept
steps.content:
Steady-state occurs when the rate of heat energy entering any section of the rod is exactly equal to the rate of heat energy leaving it. At this point, the temperature gradient ($$\frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$$) becomes constant. 

If the experiment is still in the "transient" phase (the initial warming-up period), the temperatures are still rising as the material absorbs energy to increase its own internal energy.

steps.title: Analyze the effect of time
steps.content:
If the assumption is incorrect and steady-state has **not** been reached, the temperature readings will be affected by the material's **specific heat capacity** ($c$). 

As seen in **Topic B.1 (Thermal energy transfers)**, the energy required to raise the temperature of a mass is given by:

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

During transient heating, a material with a high specific heat capacity will take longer to reach a stable temperature compared to one with a lower specific heat capacity, even if their thermal conductivities are the same.

steps.title: State the final impact
steps.content:
The final conclusion for your exam should be:

1. **Assumption:** The rod has reached steady-state conduction after 300 seconds.
2. **Effect:** If this is not true, the comparison is inaccurate because the results would reflect the "thermal inertia" (a combination of density and specific heat) of the copper and steel rather than just their thermal conductivities.

steps.title: Identify the assumption
steps.content:
In experiments involving thermal conductivity, we often focus on how heat moves *through* a material. The **Negligible Heat Loss Assumption** assumes that the rods are perfectly insulated from their surroundings. 

This means we assume that no thermal energy is lost to the air through the sides of the rod, and all energy supplied by the heater travels strictly along the length of the rod from $x_1$ to $x_2$.

steps.title: Calculate temperature gradients
steps.content:
To see why this assumption matters, let's look at the temperature drops ($\Delta T$) measured over the $6.0\text{ cm}$ distance between the thermocouples for both rods:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_A &= 85.0^{\circ}\text{C} - 75.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 10.0^{\circ}\text{C} \\ \Delta T_B &= 75.0^{\circ}\text{C} - 35.0^{\circ}\text{C} = 40.0^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned}$$

We use these values to compare how well each material conducts heat. If the Negligible Heat Loss Assumption is true, these drops are caused entirely by the internal resistance of the copper and steel.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the temperature at x2 from the temperature at x1 for both rods.

desmos.instructions: Enter the subtractions to find the temperature drops.

calculator.description: Calculate the temperature difference for Rod A and Rod B.

steps.title: Analyze the effect
steps.content:
If this assumption is incorrect and heat is actually escaping into the environment, the temperature at the second position ($T_2$) will be lower than expected. 

This "extra" cooling would make the temperature gradient appear steeper than it actually is. Consequently, we might calculate a lower thermal conductivity for the material because we would mistakenly attribute the temperature drop solely to the material's properties rather than environmental cooling. This leads to an inaccurate comparison between the copper and steel rods.

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
To earn the marks, you should state the assumption and its physical consequence:

*   **Assumption:** Negligible heat loss to the surroundings (or the rods are perfectly insulated).
*   **Explanation:** If heat is lost to the air, the temperature at $x_2$ decreases further, leading to an overestimation of the temperature gradient and an inaccurate comparison of the two materials.

steps.title: Identify the core assumption
steps.content:
In this experiment, the most critical assumption is that **steady-state heat flow** has been reached by the time the measurements are taken. 

This means we assume that the rate of energy transfer (power) from the heater into the rod has become constant and that the temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ are no longer changing with time. This aligns with the **Constant Power Transfer Assumption**, where we treat the heater's output into both Rod A and Rod B as identical and stable to allow for a fair comparison of their thermal conductivities.

steps.title: Explain the physical effect
steps.content:
If the 300-second duration is too short, the rods may still be in a **transient state**. During this phase, the energy transferred from the heater is being used to increase the internal energy (temperature) of the rod material itself, rather than just being conducted through it. 

Because copper and steel have different specific heat capacities and densities, they will warm up at different rates. If measurements are taken before steady state is reached, the temperature gradients will reflect these transient heating properties rather than the pure thermal conductivity, leading to an inaccurate and invalid comparison between the two materials.

steps.title: Relate to marking criteria
steps.content:
To earn full marks for this type of question in an IB exam, you must clearly state the assumption and its consequence:

1.  **Identification (1 mark):** State that the experiment assumes steady-state heat flow is reached by 300 s.
2.  **Explanation (1 mark):** Explain that if this is not the case, the measured temperature gradients reflect transient conduction rather than steady conduction, making the comparison inaccurate.

This concept is part of **Topic B.1 (Thermal energy transfers)**. If you are unfamiliar with how materials reach thermal equilibrium, you should review the mechanisms of thermal energy transfer.

parts.content: Calculate the current through the bulb.

$I=\frac{120.0}{60.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$I=2.0 \text{ A}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, let's identify the information provided in the question. We are given the resistance ($R$) and the operating voltage ($V$) for the light bulb:

*   Resistance, $R = 60.0 \ \Omega$
*   Voltage, $V = 120.0 \text{ V}$

steps.title: Rearrange Ohm's Law
steps.content:
From **Topic B.5 (Current and circuits)**, we know the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance is defined by Ohm's Law:

$$V = IR$$

To find the current ($I$), we need to rearrange this formula by dividing both sides by $R$:

$$I = \frac{V}{R}$$

steps.title: Calculate the current
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into our rearranged equation to solve for the current:

$$I = \frac{120.0}{60.0}$$

$$I = 2.0 \text{ A}$$

In an IB exam, showing this substitution step correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, type 120.0 / 60.0.

ti84.instructions: Enter 120.0 / 60.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the current by dividing voltage by resistance.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The final current through the bulb is $2.0 \text{ A}$. 

Make sure to include the correct unit (Amperes, A) to secure the final mark (**A1**).

parts.content: Determine the power output of the bulb.
parts.markscheme:
$P=2.0 \times 120.0$ <marks>M1</marks>
$P=240 \text{ W}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, we identify the physical quantities provided in the question. These concepts are covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

*   Resistance ($R$) = $60.0 \ \Omega$
*   Voltage ($V$) = $120.0 \text{ V}$

steps.title: Apply the power formula
steps.content:
To calculate the power output directly using voltage and resistance, we use the specific variation of the power formula found in **Topic B.5.4** of the IB syllabus:

$$P = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

This formula is derived by substituting Ohm's Law ($I = \frac{V}{R}$) into the standard power equation ($P = VI$).

steps.title: Calculate the power
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the equation to find the power ($P$):

$$\begin{aligned} P &= \frac{(120.0)^2}{60.0} \\ P &= \frac{14400}{60.0} \\ P &= 240 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

Note that since the inputs are given to three or four significant figures ($60.0$ and $120.0$), our final answer is appropriately expressed as $240 \text{ W}$ (3 s.f.).

gdc.instructions: Enter (120.0)^2 / 60.0 into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type 120.0^2 / 60.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the power output using the given voltage and resistance.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The power output of the bulb is **$240 \text{ W}$**.

In an IB exam, you would earn marks for:
1.  **M1**: Correct substitution into the power formula (even if you used $P = VI$ after finding current first).
2.  **A1**: Providing the correct final value with units.

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we first need to identify the electrical properties provided in the question. We are given the resistance ($R$) and the operating voltage ($V$) of the light bulb.

*   Resistance $R = 60.0 \ \Omega$
*   Voltage $V = 120.0 \text{ V}$

steps.title: Calculate the electric current
steps.content:
Using the method of first finding the current, we apply Ohm's Law. This is a fundamental concept found in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**. The formula for current ($I$) is:

$$I = \frac{V}{R}$$

Substituting our values:

$$I = \frac{120.0 \text{ V}}{60.0 \ \Omega} = 2.0 \text{ A}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 120.0 divided by 60.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the current through the bulb.

steps.title: Calculate the power output
steps.content:
Now that we have the current ($I = 2.0 \text{ A}$), we can calculate the power output ($P$) using the electrical power formula from **Topic B.5**:

$$P = VI$$

By substituting the voltage and the current we just found:

$$P = 120.0 \text{ V} \times 2.0 \text{ A} = 240 \text{ W}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 120.0 multiplied by 2.0 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the power output in Watts.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The power output of the bulb is **$240 \text{ W}$**. 

In an IB exam, you earn one mark (**M1**) for showing the substitution into the power formula and one mark (**A1**) for the correct final answer with units.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
To start, let's identify what we know from the question. We are given the resistance ($R$) and the operating voltage ($V$) of the light bulb. 

- Resistance $R = 60.0 \ \Omega$
- Voltage $V = 120.0 \text{ V}$

steps.title: Calculate the current
steps.content:
First, we need to find the current ($I$) flowing through the bulb. We can use Ohm's Law, which is found in **Topic B.5 Current and circuits**. 

Rearranging $V = IR$ to solve for $I$:

$$I = \frac{V}{R}$$

Substituting our values:

$$I = \frac{120.0 \text{ V}}{60.0 \ \Omega} = 2.0 \text{ A}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 120 / 60 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the current using the division operator.

steps.title: Calculate power output
steps.content:
Now that we have the current ($I = 2.0 \text{ A}$), we can calculate the power output ($P$) using the formula $P = I^2R$ from the data booklet.

$$\begin{aligned} P &= I^2 \times R \\ P &= (2.0 \text{ A})^2 \times 60.0 \ \Omega \\ P &= 4.0 \times 60.0 \\ P &= 240 \text{ W} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 2^2 * 60 and press ENTER.

steps.title: Final result
steps.content:
The power output of the bulb is $240 \text{ W}$.

In an IB exam, you would earn one mark (**M1**) for showing the substitution into a correct power formula and one mark (**A1**) for the final answer with units.

![Circuit Diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-3a061a45-1bd9-416b-b6ac-74648f136454-1771937587718/9e6b8ae8-21db-49cd-8ca5-6ff654dc0990.jpeg)

| | $\frac{\text{Current in } X}{\text{Current in } Y}$ | $\frac{\text{Drift speed in } X}{\text{Drift speed in } Y}$ |
|:---|:---|:---|
| A | 1 | 2 |
| B | 1 | 4 |
| C | 4 | 1 |
| D | 4 | 4 |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.5.2 Electrical Current and Voltage
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the fact that in a series circuit the current is the same through both wires, and relates current to drift speed via I = nqAvd.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.5.3 Resistance and Resistivity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Wire diameter (cross-sectional area) affects resistance and current density; comparing diameters connects to resistivity/resistance and how geometry influences conduction quantities.

options.markscheme: In series, the current is the same in both wires so $I_X/I_Y=1$ (this matches), but drift speed satisfies $I=nqAv$ so $v\propto 1/A$ for fixed $I$; with $d_Y=2d_X$, $A_Y=4A_X$ hence $v_X/v_Y=A_Y/A_X=4$, not 2.

In series, the current is the same so $I_X/I_Y=1$. Using $I=nqAv$ (same material so $n$ and $q$ are the same), with $I$ the same, $v\propto 1/A$. Since $d_Y=2d_X$, $A_Y=4A_X$, hence $v_X/v_Y=(I/A_X)/(I/A_Y)=A_Y/A_X=4$.
In series, the current is the same in both wires, so $I_X/I_Y\neq 4$ (it is 1). Also, with $d_Y=2d_X$, $A_Y=4A_X$ and for equal current $v_X/v_Y=4$, not 1.
In series, the current is the same in both wires so $I_X/I_Y=1$, not 4. While $v_X/v_Y=4$ is correct (since $A_Y=4A_X$), the current ratio is incorrect, so this option is not correct.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the electrical resistance of a metallic wire by measuring its voltage $V$ and current $I$. The resistance $R$ is calculated using Ohm's Law:

$$
R=\frac{V}{I}
$$

The percentage uncertainty in the voltage measurement is 1.0%, and the percentage uncertainty in the current measurement is $0.5\%$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.5 Current and circuits > B.5.3 Resistance and Resistivity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.5.3 Resistance and Resistivity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Core task is determining resistance using Ohm’s law (R=V/I) and discussing reducing uncertainty in resistance measurements for a metallic wire.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.5 Current and circuits > B.5.2 Electrical Current and Voltage
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.5.2 Electrical Current and Voltage
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses measured voltage and current values (V and I) and their percentage uncertainties as inputs to calculate R and its uncertainty.

parts.content:
Using $R=\frac{V}{I}$, show that the percentage uncertainty in $R$ is given by:

Percentage uncertainty in $R$ = Percentage uncertainty in $V$ + Percentage uncertainty in $I$
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly derive the relationship:

Percentage uncertainty in $R$ = Percentage uncertainty in $V$ + Percentage uncertainty in $I$ by noting that $R \propto \frac{V}{I}$. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

- Clearly state that uncertainties add when values are divided or multiplied. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

parts.content: The student records $V=12.0 \text{ V}$ and $I=2.40 \text{ A}$. Calculate the value of $R$ and its percentage uncertainty.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $R$: $R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{12.0}{2.40} = 5.00 \, \Omega$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Calculate the percentage uncertainty: $\text{Percentage uncertainty in } R = 1.0\% + 0.5\% = 1.5\%$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Calculate the resistance value
steps.content:
To find the resistance $R$, we use the values for voltage $V$ and current $I$ provided in the question and substitute them into Ohm's Law:

$$\begin{aligned} R &= \frac{V}{I} \\ R &= \frac{12.0 \text{ V}}{2.40 \text{ A}} \\ R &= 5.00 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Since both $V$ and $I$ are given to three significant figures, we express our answer as $5.00 \, \Omega$ to maintain consistency.

ti84.instructions: Enter 12.0 divided by 2.40 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the nominal value of resistance.

steps.title: Sum the percentage uncertainties
steps.content:
When quantities are multiplied or divided, the IB rule for uncertainty propagation is to add the individual percentage uncertainties together.

$$\begin{aligned} \% \text{ uncertainty in } R &= \% \text{ uncertainty in } V + \% \text{ uncertainty in } I \\ \% \text{ uncertainty in } R &= 1.0\% + 0.5\% \\ \% \text{ uncertainty in } R &= 1.5\% \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.0 + 0.5 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Add the percentage uncertainties.

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the voltage measurement is 1.0%
percentage uncertainty in the current measurement is $0.5\%$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The final value for the resistance is $5.00 \, \Omega$ with a percentage uncertainty of $1.5\%$.

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the correct calculation of $R$ and one mark for correctly summing the uncertainties.

steps.title: Calculate nominal resistance
steps.content:
First, we use the recorded values for voltage and current to find the central value of the resistance using Ohm's Law provided in the question:

$$R = \frac{V}{I}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$R = \frac{12.0 \text{ V}}{2.40 \text{ A}} = 5.00 \, \Omega$$

This calculation provides the first mark as per the markscheme.

ti84.instructions: Enter 12.0 divided by 2.40.

calculator.description: Verify the nominal resistance value.

steps.title: Convert to absolute uncertainties
steps.content:
To find the extreme values, we must convert the percentage uncertainties of $V$ and $I$ into absolute uncertainties ($\Delta V$ and $\Delta I$).

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta V &= 1.0\% \times 12.0 \text{ V} = 0.12 \text{ V} \\ \Delta I &= 0.5\% \times 2.40 \text{ A} = 0.012 \text{ A} \end{aligned}$$

This means the voltage range is $12.0 \pm 0.12 \text{ V}$ and the current range is $2.40 \pm 0.012 \text{ A}$.

ti84.instructions:
1. Multiply 0.01 by 12.0. 
2. Multiply 0.005 by 2.40.

calculator.description: Calculate absolute uncertainties.

steps.title: Calculate extreme resistance values
steps.content:
Using the **Extreme Values Method**, we find the maximum and minimum possible resistance values:

- To get $R_{max}$, we divide the largest possible voltage by the smallest possible current.
- To get $R_{min}$, we divide the smallest possible voltage by the largest possible current.

$$\begin{aligned} R_{max} &= \frac{12.0 + 0.12}{2.40 - 0.012} = \frac{12.12}{2.388} \approx 5.0754 \, \Omega \\ R_{min} &= \frac{12.0 - 0.12}{2.40 + 0.012} = \frac{11.88}{2.412} \approx 4.9254 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the max voltage by min current and min voltage by max current.

calculator.description: Find the maximum and minimum values of R.

steps.title: Determine final percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
The absolute uncertainty in $R$ ($\Delta R$) is half the range between the extreme values. We then calculate the percentage uncertainty.

$$\Delta R = \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{2} = \frac{5.0754 - 4.9254}{2} = 0.075 \, \Omega$$

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty} = \frac{0.075}{5.00} \times 100\% = 1.5\%$$

This result earns the second mark according to the markscheme. Note that for small percentages, this method aligns perfectly with the shortcut of simply adding percentage uncertainties ($1.0\% + 0.5\% = 1.5\%$).

ti84.instructions: Divide the uncertainty 0.075 by the nominal 5.00, then multiply by 100.

calculator.description: Calculate the final percentage uncertainty from the range.

parts.content: Outline two ways to reduce the percentage uncertainty in the value of $R$.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks> 1 mark </marks> for each of the following, <marks> 2 marks max </marks>:

- Use a higher-precision voltmeter and ammeter to reduce the measurement uncertainties for $V$ and $I$. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Measure a larger voltage or current to minimize the relative effect of small uncertainties. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Perform multiple measurements for $V$ and $I$, then calculate an average to reduce random errors. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze uncertainty propagation
steps.content:
To reduce the percentage uncertainty in the resistance $R$, we first need to understand how it is calculated. Since $R = \frac{V}{I}$, the percentage uncertainty in $R$ is the sum of the percentage uncertainties of the voltage and current measurements:

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty in } R = \text{Percentage uncertainty in } V + \text{Percentage uncertainty in } I$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{Total percentage uncertainty} = 1.0\% + 0.5\% = 1.5\%$$

To reduce this total, we must reduce the individual percentage uncertainties of $V$ and $I$.

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the voltage measurement is 1.0%
percentage uncertainty in the current measurement is 0.5%

steps.title: Use higher-precision instruments
steps.content:
One way to reduce uncertainty is to replace the current equipment with a **higher-precision voltmeter and ammeter**. 

Instruments with higher precision have a smaller absolute uncertainty (for example, a digital voltmeter that displays more decimal places). Since percentage uncertainty is calculated as:

$$\text{Percentage uncertainty} = \frac{\text{Absolute uncertainty}}{\text{Measured value}} \times 100\%$$

Reducing the absolute uncertainty (the numerator) directly decreases the percentage uncertainty in the measurements of $V$ and $I$.

steps.title: Measure larger values
steps.content:
A second way is to **measure a larger voltage or current**. 

If you use the same instruments but increase the magnitude of the values being measured (the denominator in the percentage uncertainty formula), the fixed absolute uncertainty of the instrument becomes a smaller fraction of the total measurement. This effectively minimizes the relative effect of the instrument's uncertainty on the final value of $R$.

steps.title: Define percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
To reduce the uncertainty in resistance $R$, we first need to understand how percentage uncertainty is calculated for any measurement $x$. It is the ratio of the absolute uncertainty $\Delta x$ to the measured value $x$, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 100\%$$

In this experiment, the total percentage uncertainty in $R$ is the sum of the percentage uncertainties in $V$ and $I$.

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the voltage measurement is 1.0%
percentage uncertainty in the current measurement is 0.5%

steps.title: Method 1: Use larger magnitudes
steps.content:
The most effective way to reduce percentage uncertainty without changing equipment is to **measure larger values of voltage and current**. 

Most measuring instruments have a fixed **absolute uncertainty** (for example, a voltmeter might always be accurate to $\pm 0.1\text{ V}$). By increasing the voltage $V$ being measured, this fixed absolute uncertainty becomes a much smaller fraction of the total measurement. 

For example, if $\Delta V = 0.1\text{ V}$:
- At $V = 1\text{ V}$, the percentage uncertainty is $\frac{0.1}{1} \times 100 = 10\%$.
- At $V = 10\text{ V}$, the percentage uncertainty drops to $\frac{0.1}{10} \times 100 = 1\%$.

ti84.instructions: Divide the absolute uncertainty by the measurement and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the percentage uncertainty formula for a larger magnitude.

calculator.description: Compare percentage uncertainties for different magnitudes.

steps.title: Method 2: Higher precision instruments
steps.content:
Another way to reduce uncertainty is to **use a higher-precision voltmeter and ammeter**. 

High-precision instruments have a smaller absolute uncertainty (a smaller $\Delta V$ or $\Delta I$). Referring back to our formula:

$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{\Delta x}{x} \times 100\%$$

By choosing a device where $\Delta x$ is smaller, the resulting percentage uncertainty for the same measured value $x$ will decrease.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks, you should clearly state two distinct strategies:

1.  **Measure larger values of $V$ and $I$**: This ensures the fixed absolute uncertainty of the meters represents a smaller proportion of the total reading.
2.  **Use higher-precision meters**: This directly reduces the absolute uncertainty $(\Delta V$ and $\Delta I)$ of the measurements.

Note: You could also mention taking multiple readings and calculating an average to reduce the effect of random errors.

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
To reduce the percentage uncertainty in the resistance $R$, we need to address the random errors that occur during the measurement of voltage ($V$) and current ($I$). 

In the IB curriculum (Topic B.5), resistance is defined as:
$$R = \frac{V}{I}$$

When we use the **Statistical Averaging** approach, we aim to minimize the impact of these random fluctuations by collecting more data points.

steps.title: Perform multiple trials
steps.content:
The first way to reduce uncertainty is to **repeat the measurements multiple times** for the same set of conditions. 

Instead of taking a single reading for $V$ and $I$, you should conduct several trials (e.g., 5 trials). This allows you to identify any anomalous results (outliers) that might have been caused by a momentary fluctuation in the circuit or a reading error.

highlights.text: Perform multiple measurements for V and I, then calculate an average to reduce random errors.

steps.title: Calculate the mean
steps.content:
Once you have multiple readings, you calculate the **mean (average) value** for both $V$ and $I$. 

For example, if you have $n$ measurements of current, the mean current $I_{mean}$ is:
$$I_{mean} = \frac{I_1 + I_2 + ... + I_n}{n}$$

Statistically, the mean is more likely to be closer to the true value because random errors (which can be either too high or too low) tend to cancel each other out when averaged.

gdc.instructions: Enter the numbers into a list and use the mean() function.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] [1], enter values in L1. Then [STAT] [CALC] [1] for 1-Var Stats.

desmos.instructions: Type 'mean' followed by the list of numbers in parentheses.

calculator.description: Calculate the mean of three current trials: 0.48A, 0.52A, and 0.50A.

steps.title: Use graphical analysis
steps.content:
A second way to apply statistical averaging is to **measure a range of different values** for $V$ and $I$ and plot a graph of $V$ against $I$. 

By drawing a **line of best fit**, you are effectively averaging the relationship across all data points. The gradient of this line represents the resistance $R$. Because the line is influenced by every data point, it reduces the impact of the uncertainty associated with any single measurement pair.

steps.title: Final summary
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme and the statistical approach, the two ways to reduce percentage uncertainty are:

1.  **Perform multiple trials** for each measurement and calculate the **average (mean)** to reduce the effect of random errors.
2.  **Take multiple data points** across a range of values and use a **graphical method** (line of best fit) to determine the resistance.

parts.content: The student performs three separate experiments and obtains the following values for $R$: $5.05 \, \Omega$, $5.10 \, \Omega$, and $5.00 \, \Omega$. Calculate the mean resistance and the absolute uncertainty based on the range of the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the mean value: $\text{Mean } R = \frac{5.05 + 5.10 + 5.00}{3} = 5.05 \, \Omega$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Calculate the range-based uncertainty: $\text{Uncertainty } = \frac{\text{Range}}{2} = \frac{5.10 - 5.00}{2} = 0.05 \, \Omega$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Final result: $R = 5.05 \pm 0.05 \, \Omega$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Calculate the mean resistance
steps.content:
First, we find the arithmetic mean (average) of the three experimental values. To do this, add the values together and divide by the number of trials ($n = 3$).

$$\text{Mean } R = \frac{5.05 + 5.10 + 5.00}{3} = 5.05 \, \Omega$$

Calculating the mean is the first step in reporting a central value for repeated measurements. You earn **1 mark** for this correct calculation.

gdc.instructions: Enter (5.05 + 5.10 + 5.00) / 3 into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the average of the three resistance values.

steps.title: Calculate the absolute uncertainty
steps.content:
Using the **Mean and Half-Range Method**, we determine the absolute uncertainty by finding half of the difference between the maximum and minimum values in the dataset.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Max Value} &= 5.10 \, \Omega \\ \text{Min Value} &= 5.00 \, \Omega \\ \Delta R &= \frac{\text{Max} - \text{Min}}{2} \\ \Delta R &= \frac{5.10 - 5.00}{2} = 0.05 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$

Identifying the range and dividing by two earns you **1 mark**.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate half of the range.

highlights.text: absolute uncertainty based on the range of the data

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
Finally, combine the mean value and the absolute uncertainty into the standard format: $\text{Value} \pm \text{Uncertainty}$.

$$R = 5.05 \pm 0.05 \, \Omega$$

Ensuring both the value and the uncertainty are expressed with the same precision (number of decimal places) is key to earning the final **1 mark**.

parts.markscheme:
- Calculate total resistance:
$$ R_{\text{total}} = r_{\text{internal}} + r_{\text{external}} = 1 + 4 = 5 \, \Omega $$ <marks>M1</marks>

steps.title: Identify circuit parameters
steps.content:
First, we identify the values given in the question. The circuit consists of a battery with a specific electromotive force (emf) and internal resistance connected to an external resistor. This relates to **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

*   Emf ($ε$) = $9 \text{ V}$
*   Internal resistance ($r$) = $1 \, \Omega$
*   External resistance ($R$) = $4 \, \Omega$

steps.title: Calculate total resistance
steps.content:
In this approach, we treat the internal resistance and external load as two resistors connected in series. We calculate the total resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$) by summing them.

$$R_{\text{total}} = r + R$$

Substituting the given values:

$$R_{\text{total}} = 1 + 4 = 5 \, \Omega$$

ti84.instructions: Type 1 + 4 and press ENTER

calculator.description: Calculate sum of resistances

steps.title: Apply Ohm's Law
steps.content:
Now we apply Ohm's Law to the entire circuit to find the current ($I$). The total potential difference driving the current is the emf ($ε$).

$$I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

$$I = \frac{9}{5}$$

ti84.instructions: Type 9 / 5 and press ENTER

calculator.description: Calculate current

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the division gives the current flowing through the circuit.

$$I = 1.8 \, \text{A}$$

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, we list the values provided in the question for the electromotive force (emf), internal resistance, and external load resistance:

*   Emf: $\varepsilon = 9 \, \text{V}$
*   Internal resistance: $r = 1 \, \Omega$
*   External resistance: $R = 4 \, \Omega$

steps.title: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law
steps.content:
Using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL), we know that for a single closed loop, the sum of the emf is equal to the sum of the potential drops across all resistors. 

The potential drops occur across the internal resistance ($Ir$) and the external load ($IR$). This relates to **Topic B.5.5 (Electric Cells)**:

$$\varepsilon = Ir + IR$$

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Now we substitute the known values into the equation to set up a linear equation for the current $I$:

$$9 = I(1) + I(4)$$

steps.title: Solve for current
steps.content:
Factor out $I$ and solve the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 9 &= I(1 + 4) \\ 9 &= 5I \\ I &= \frac{9}{5} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 9 divided by 5

calculator.description: Calculate the final current

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Performing the division gives the final current:

$$I = 1.8 \, \text{A}$$

parts.content: Find the terminal voltage across the load resistor during operation.
parts.markscheme:
### Method 1

- Use the formula for potential difference across a resistor ($V = IR$): <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute the values to find the terminal voltage: $V = 1.8 \times 4 = 7.2 \, \text{V}$ <marks>A1</marks>

### Method 2

- Calculate total resistance: $R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{internal}} + R_{\text{external}} = 1 + 4 = 5 \, \Omega$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Use the formula for terminal voltage ($V_{\text{terminal}} = \text{emf} - I R_{\text{internal}}$) and substitute: $V_{\text{terminal}} = 9 - (1.8 \times 1) = 7.2 \, \text{V}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award M1 for correct formula/method. Award A1 for correct substitution and working to find 7.2 V.</note>

steps.title: Identify given circuit parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the **External Ohm's Law Approach**, we first list the known values from the problem:
- Electromotive force (emf), $\varepsilon = 9 \text{ V}$
- Internal resistance, $r = 1 \, \Omega$
- External load resistance, $R = 4 \, \Omega$

This setup represents a simple series circuit where the battery's internal resistance and the load resistor are in series. This topic is covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

steps.title: Calculate total circuit resistance
steps.content:
Since the internal resistance $r$ and the external load $R$ are in series, we calculate the total resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$) by summing them:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R + r$$

Substituting the values:
$$R_{\text{total}} = 4 \, \Omega + 1 \, \Omega = 5 \, \Omega$$

gdc.instructions: Add the two resistance values on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 + 1 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total resistance by adding the external and internal resistances.

steps.title: Find the total current
steps.content:
Using the definition of current for a whole circuit, we divide the total emf by the total resistance:

$$I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

$$I = \frac{9 \text{ V}}{5 \, \Omega} = 1.8 \text{ A}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 9 / 5 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Divide the emf (9V) by the total resistance (5 ohms) to find the current.

steps.title: Apply Ohm's Law
steps.content:
The terminal voltage ($V$) is the potential difference specifically across the external load resistor. We find this by applying Ohm's Law ($V = IR$) to the external resistor $R$:

$$V = I \times R$$

$$V = 1.8 \text{ A} \times 4 \, \Omega = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the current value by the resistance value.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.8 * 4 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Multiply the current (1.8 A) by the external load resistance (4 ohms).

highlights.text: Use the formula for potential difference across a resistor (V = IR)

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The terminal voltage across the load resistor is $7.2 \text{ V}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** (M1) for correctly identifying the method (either calculating the total resistance or using $V=IR$) and **1 mark** (A1) for the correct final value of $7.2 \text{ V}$.

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the **EMF and Internal Resistance Approach**, we first list the known values from the question:
- Electromotive force (emf), $\varepsilon = 9\text{ V}$
- Internal resistance, $r = 1 \, \Omega$
- External load resistance, $R = 4 \, \Omega$

This problem falls under **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**, which covers how real power sources (cells with internal resistance) behave in a circuit.

steps.title: Calculate total circuit resistance
steps.content:
Since the internal resistance and the external load are in series, we calculate the total resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$) by adding them together:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R + r$$
$$R_{\text{total}} = 4 \, \Omega + 1 \, \Omega = 5 \, \Omega$$

This is a necessary first step to find the current flowing through the battery.

steps.title: Find the circuit current
steps.content:
Now, we use Ohm's law for the entire circuit to find the current ($I$). The total voltage provided is the emf ($\varepsilon$):

$$I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R_{\text{total}}}$$
$$I = \frac{9\text{ V}}{5 \, \Omega} = 1.8\text{ A}$$

This current flows through both the internal resistance and the external load.

gdc.instructions: Enter 9 / 5 in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press 9, then the division key, then 5, and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the current by dividing EMF by total resistance.

steps.title: Determine terminal voltage
steps.content:
The terminal voltage ($V$) is the potential difference actually available to the external circuit. It is equal to the emf minus the 'lost volts' across the internal resistance ($Ir$):

$$V = \varepsilon - Ir$$
$$V = 9\text{ V} - (1.8\text{ A} \times 1 \, \Omega)$$
$$V = 9\text{ V} - 1.8\text{ V} = 7.2\text{ V}$$

This confirms that because of the internal resistance, the voltage delivered to the vehicle's systems is slightly less than the battery's rated emf.

ti84.instructions: Press 9, then the subtraction key, then (1.8 * 1), and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Subtract the internal voltage drop from the EMF.

highlights.text: Use the formula for terminal voltage ($V_{\text{terminal}} = \text{emf} - I R_{\text{internal}}$)

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
To find the terminal voltage, we first need to list the values provided in the problem. The terminal voltage is the voltage across the external load resistor when the circuit is closed.

- Emf ($\varepsilon$) = $9\text{ V}$
- Internal resistance ($r$) = $1 \, \Omega$
- External load resistance ($R$) = $4 \, \Omega$

steps.title: Calculate total circuit resistance
steps.content:
The internal resistance and the external load resistor are connected in series. Therefore, the total resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$) of the circuit is the sum of these two resistances.

$$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{total}} &= R + r \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 4 \, \Omega + 1 \, \Omega \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 5 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 + 1 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Summing the external and internal resistances to find total resistance.

steps.title: Apply Potential Divider Rule
steps.content:
The Potential Divider Rule allows us to calculate the voltage across a specific resistor in a series circuit directly. The terminal voltage ($V$) is the voltage dropped across the external load ($R$). It is calculated as the ratio of the load resistance to the total resistance, multiplied by the total emf.

This concept is a core part of **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

$$V = \varepsilon \times \frac{R}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

steps.title: Calculate terminal voltage
steps.content:
Substitute the known values into the potential divider formula:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= 9 \text{ V} \times \left( \frac{4 \, \Omega}{5 \, \Omega} \right) \\ V &= 9 \times 0.8 \\ V &= 7.2 \text{ V} \end{aligned}$$

The terminal voltage across the load resistor is $7.2\text{ V}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 9 * (4 / 5) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter 9 * (4 / 5) to find the terminal voltage.

calculator.description: Calculating the terminal voltage using the potential divider ratio.

parts.content: Explain the impact of internal resistance on the performance and efficiency of the battery in electric vehicles.
parts.markscheme:
- Internal resistance causes a potential drop across the battery itself ($V_{\text{int}} = IR_{\text{int}}$), which reduces the terminal voltage available to the load. <marks>R1</marks>

- As internal resistance increases, more energy is dissipated as heat in the battery ($P_{\text{wasted}} = I^2R_{\text{int}}$), reducing the efficiency of energy transfer to the load. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award at most 1 mark if internal resistance is only stated to reduce battery life/capacity without explaining the mechanism.</note>

steps.title: Define the circuit model
steps.content: To understand the impact of internal resistance, we model the battery as a perfect source of electromotive force (emf, $\varepsilon$) connected in series with an internal resistor ($r$). When the electric vehicle draws current ($I$) from the battery, this current must flow through the internal resistance.

steps.title: Impact on performance (Voltage)
steps.content:
As current flows, some potential difference is lost across the internal resistance. This is often called "lost volts" and is calculated by Ohm's Law as $V_{lost} = Ir$. This drop reduces the terminal voltage ($V_{term}$) available to power the vehicle's motor and systems:

$$V_{term} = \varepsilon - Ir$$

A lower terminal voltage means the battery delivers less voltage to the load than its rated emf, which can reduce the performance (speed or torque) of the electric vehicle.

steps.title: Impact on efficiency (Heat)
steps.content:
The internal resistance also causes energy to be dissipated as heat inside the battery. The power wasted due to this heating effect is given by:

$$P_{lost} = I^2 r$$

This energy is lost as heat rather than being used to drive the vehicle, which directly reduces the **efficiency** of the energy transfer from the battery to the motor.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
In summary, internal resistance has two negative impacts:
1. **Performance**: It lowers the terminal voltage available to the load.
2. **Efficiency**: It causes energy to be wasted as internal heat.

steps.title: Define circuit parameters
steps.content:
To understand the impact of internal resistance, we will use the **Numerical Illustration** method with the values provided in the question specification. We identify the electromotive force (emf), internal resistance ($r$), and load resistance ($R$).

Given values:
- $\varepsilon = 9 \, \text{V}$
- $r = 1 \, \Omega$
- $R = 4 \, \Omega$

steps.title: Calculate circuit current
steps.content:
First, we determine the current flowing through the circuit. The internal resistance ($r$) and the load resistance ($R$) are in series, so we use the formula for current from **Topic B.5**:

$$I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R + r}$$

Substituting our values:

$$I = \frac{9}{4 + 1} = \frac{9}{5} = 1.8 \, \text{A}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 9 divided by (4 + 1)

calculator.description: Calculate current I

steps.title: Analyze terminal voltage drop
steps.content:
Internal resistance causes a "lost produced voltage" inside the battery. We calculate the terminal voltage ($V_{\text{term}}$) reaching the electric vehicle's systems using the equation $V = \varepsilon - Ir$:

$$V_{\text{term}} = 9 - (1.8 \times 1) = 9 - 1.8 = 7.2 \, \text{V}$$

**Impact on Performance:** Instead of the full $9 \, \text{V}$, the vehicle only receives $7.2 \, \text{V}$. This voltage drop reduces the power available to the motor and other systems, limiting the vehicle's maximum speed or acceleration.

gdc.instructions: Subtract product of current and internal resistance from emf

ti84.instructions: Subtract product of current and internal resistance from emf

calculator.description: Calculate terminal voltage

highlights.text: Internal resistance causes a potential drop across the battery itself

steps.title: Analyze power and efficiency
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the power wasted as heat inside the battery due to internal resistance using $P = I^2 r$:

$$P_{\text{wasted}} = (1.8)^2 \times 1 = 3.24 \, \text{W}$$

The total power generated by the emf is $P_{\text{total}} = I\varepsilon = 1.8 \times 9 = 16.2 \, \text{W}$.

We can quantify the efficiency $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{16.2 - 3.24}{16.2} \approx 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%$$

**Impact on Efficiency:** $20\%$ of the energy is dissipated as heat within the battery, reducing the driving range and requiring better cooling systems.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1.8^2 * 1 for wasted power, then (16.2 - 3.24)/16.2 for efficiency

ti84.instructions: Calculate 1.8^2 * 1 for wasted power, then (16.2 - 3.24)/16.2 for efficiency

calculator.description: Calculate wasted power and efficiency

highlights.text: As internal resistance increases, more energy is dissipated as heat

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
To summarize the explanation for full marks:

1.  **Voltage Drop:** The internal resistance creates a potential drop ($Ir$), reducing the terminal voltage available to the load ($7.2 \, \text{V}$ instead of $9 \, \text{V}$).
2.  **Efficiency Loss:** Energy is dissipated as heat ($I^2 r$), which wastes power ($3.24 \, \text{W}$) and lowers the overall efficiency of the energy transfer.

parts.content: Calculate the total resistance in the circuit.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that resistors are in series so total resistance is sum of individual resistances <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate total resistance: $R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 20 \, \Omega$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the circuit type
steps.content:
The question specifies that the circuit contains **three resistors connected in series**. This is a key piece of information because the way we calculate total resistance depends entirely on whether the components are in series or parallel.

In **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**, you learn that for resistors in series, there is only one path for the current to flow, meaning the total resistance is simply the sum of all individual resistances.

steps.title: Apply the series formula
steps.content:
To find the total resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$), we use the standard series configuration formula:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

Recognizing that you need to sum these values is the first step toward earning marks on this problem.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given values into our equation:

$$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{total}} &= 4\,\Omega + 6\,\Omega + 10\,\Omega \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 20\,\Omega \end{aligned}$$

This final calculation gives us the total opposition to current flow in the circuit.

gdc.instructions: Enter the sum of the resistors in the main calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, type the sum of the resistors and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the sum into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the three resistances.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The total resistance in the circuit is **$20\,\Omega$**.

According to the markscheme:
*   You earn **1 mark (M1)** for showing the sum of the resistances.
*   You earn **1 mark (A1)** for the correct final answer of $20\,\Omega$.

parts.content: Determine the current flowing through the circuit.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that total current can be found using Ohm's Law: $I = \frac{V}{R}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Correct substitution and calculation: $I = \frac{12 \, \text{V}}{20 \, \Omega} = 0.6 \, \text{A}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate total resistance
steps.content:
In a series circuit, the current must pass through every resistor. Therefore, the total equivalent resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$) is simply the sum of the individual resistances. This concept is covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

$$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{total}} &= R_1 + R_2 + R_3 \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 4 \, \Omega + 6 \, \Omega + 10 \, \Omega \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 20 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply Ohm's Law
steps.content:
To find the current ($I$) flowing through the entire circuit, we apply Ohm's Law to the whole circuit. We use the battery voltage and the total resistance we just calculated.

$$\begin{aligned} I &= \frac{V}{R_{\text{total}}} \\ I &= \frac{12 \, \text{V}}{20 \, \Omega} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Recognize that total current can be found using Ohm's Law: $I = \frac{V}{R}$

steps.title: Solve with a calculator
steps.content: We can perform this division quickly using a calculator to find the final current value.

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 / 20 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the current by dividing the voltage by the total resistance.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The current flowing through the circuit is:

$$I = 0.6 \, \text{A}$$

In the IB markscheme, you receive 1 mark for the correct substitution into Ohm's Law and 1 mark for the correct final answer with units.

steps.title: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law
steps.content:
In a series circuit, **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** states that the total electromotive force (EMF) provided by the power source is equal to the sum of the potential differences (voltage drops) across each component in the loop. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

Mathematically, for three resistors in series, we write:
$$V = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$$

steps.title: Express voltage using current
steps.content:
Since the resistors are connected in series, the same current $I$ flows through each of them. We use the fundamental definition of resistance (Ohm's Law) to replace each voltage drop $V_n$ with the product of current and resistance ($IR_n$):

$$V = IR_1 + IR_2 + IR_3$$

Factoring out the current $I$, we get a linear equation:
$$V = I(R_1 + R_2 + R_3)$$ 

This shows that the total EMF is equal to the current multiplied by the sum of the resistances.

steps.title: Solve for the current
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values from the question into our equation to solve for $I$:

$$\begin{aligned} 12 &= I(4 + 6 + 10) \\ 12 &= 20I \\ I &= \frac{12}{20} \end{aligned}$$

Calculating this gives the final current in Amperes ($A$).

gdc.instructions: Divide 12 by the sum of the resistances (20).

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 / (4 + 6 + 10) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the current by dividing the total voltage by the total resistance.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The current flowing through the circuit is:

$$I = 0.6 \, \text{A}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for recognizing the relationship between current, voltage, and total resistance, and **1 mark** for the correct final value with units.

parts.content: Find the voltage drop across each resistor.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate voltage drop across $R_1$: $V_1 = 0.6 \times 4 = 2.4 \, \text{V}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate voltage drop across $R_2$: $V_2 = 0.6 \times 6 = 3.6 \, \text{V}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate voltage drop across $R_3$: $V_3 = 0.6 \times 10 = 6.0 \, \text{V}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Calculate total resistance
steps.content:
In a series circuit, the total resistance ($R_{\text{total}}$) is simply the sum of the individual resistances. This concept is covered in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**.

$$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{total}} &= R_1 + R_2 + R_3 \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 4\,\Omega + 6\,\Omega + 10\,\Omega \\ R_{\text{total}} &= 20\,\Omega \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 + 6 + 10 on the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 4 + 6 + 10 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the resistances.

steps.title: Calculate total current
steps.content:
Next, we use Ohm's Law ($V = IR$) to find the total current ($I$) flowing through the circuit. In a series circuit, this current is the same through every component.

$$\begin{aligned} I &= \frac{V}{R_{\text{total}}} \\ I &= \frac{12\,\text{V}}{20\,\Omega} \\ I &= 0.6\,\text{A} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 / 20 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 12 / 20 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Divide the battery voltage by the total resistance.

steps.title: Find individual voltage drops
steps.content:
Now we multiply the total current by each individual resistance to find the voltage drop across each resistor:

For $R_1$:
$$V_1 = I \times R_1 = 0.6\,\text{A} \times 4\,\Omega = 2.4\,\text{V}$$

For $R_2$:
$$V_2 = I \times R_2 = 0.6\,\text{A} \times 6\,\Omega = 3.6\,\text{V}$$

For $R_3$:
$$V_3 = I \times R_3 = 0.6\,\text{A} \times 10\,\Omega = 6.0\,\text{V}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.6 * 4, then 0.6 * 6, then 0.6 * 10, pressing ENTER after each.

calculator.description: Calculate each voltage drop using V = I * R.

steps.title: Calculate the total resistance
steps.content:
To use the potential divider approach, we first need to determine the total resistance of the circuit. Since the resistors are connected in series, we simply add their individual resistances together:

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

Substituting the given values:

$$R_{\text{total}} = 4 \, \Omega + 6 \, \Omega + 10 \, \Omega = 20 \, \Omega$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 4 + 6 + 10 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 4 + 6 + 10 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the sum of the resistances.

steps.title: Find voltage across R1
steps.content:
The potential divider formula allows us to find the voltage drop across a specific resistor by comparing its resistance to the total resistance:

$$V_1 = V_{\text{total}} \times \frac{R_1}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

Using the battery voltage and the resistances we found:

$$V_1 = 12 \, \text{V} \times \frac{4 \, \Omega}{20 \, \Omega} = 2.4 \, \text{V}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 * (4 / 20) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 12 * (4 / 20) in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the voltage drop for the first resistor.

steps.title: Find voltage across R2
steps.content:
Repeat the potential divider calculation for the second resistor:

$$V_2 = V_{\text{total}} \times \frac{R_2}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

$$V_2 = 12 \, \text{V} \times \frac{6 \, \Omega}{20 \, \Omega} = 3.6 \, \text{V}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 * (6 / 20) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 12 * (6 / 20) in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the voltage drop for the second resistor.

steps.title: Find voltage across R3
steps.content:
Finally, calculate the voltage drop for the third resistor:

$$V_3 = V_{\text{total}} \times \frac{R_3}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

$$V_3 = 12 \, \text{V} \times \frac{10 \, \Omega}{20 \, \Omega} = 6.0 \, \text{V}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 12 * (10 / 20) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 12 * (10 / 20) in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the voltage drop for the third resistor.

steps.title: Verify the results
steps.content:
In a series circuit, Kirchhoff's Loop Rule states that the sum of the voltage drops must equal the total EMF provided by the battery. Let's check our work:

$$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{sum}} &= V_1 + V_2 + V_3 \\ V_{\text{sum}} &= 2.4 \, \text{V} + 3.6 \, \text{V} + 6.0 \, \text{V} \\ V_{\text{sum}} &= 12.0 \, \text{V} \end{aligned}$$

This matches our 12 V battery, confirming our results are correct.

parts.content: Describe how the resistance changes with increasing length.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the resistance increases linearly as the length increases. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: Explain, using physics principles, why the resistance is expected to depend on the length of the wire.
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula $R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$ where $\rho$ is the resistivity and $A$ is the cross-sectional area. <marks>M1</marks>
- State that since resistivity and cross-sectional area are constant for a uniform wire, the resistance $R$ is directly proportional to length $L$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the resistivity formula
steps.content:
To understand how resistance ($R$) relates to the physical properties of a wire, we use the resistivity formula found in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

In this equation:
- $\rho$ is the resistivity of the material.
- $L$ is the length of the wire.
- $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the wire.

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content:
The question specifies that the student uses a "constant wire of uniform cross-sectional area." This gives us two crucial pieces of information:
1. Using a "constant wire" means the material (and thus the resistivity, $\rho$) remains the same.
2. "Uniform cross-sectional area" means the area ($A$) is constant throughout the investigation.

highlights.text: constant wire of uniform cross-sectional area

steps.title: Determine the relationship
steps.content:
Since both $\rho$ and $A$ are constant, the term $\frac{\rho}{A}$ is a constant value. 

We can rewrite the relationship as:
$$R = \left(\frac{\rho}{A}\right) \times L$$

This shows that the resistance $R$ is **directly proportional** to the length $L$. As the length increases, there is a greater distance for the charge carriers to travel, resulting in more collisions and higher resistance.

gdc.instructions: In a spreadsheet, enter lengths in Column A and resistances in Column B. In Column C, calculate B/A.

ti84.instructions: Divide the resistance values by their corresponding lengths to see if the ratio is approximately constant.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the ratios for each data point.

calculator.description: Verification of direct proportionality (R/L ratio)

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Therefore, according to the principle $R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$, resistance is expected to be proportional to length because for a uniform wire, the resistivity and cross-sectional area do not change.

**Marking Breakdown:**
- **1 Mark**: Correctly identifying or stating the formula $R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$.
- **1 Mark**: Explaining that $R$ is directly proportional to $L$ because $\rho$ and $A$ are constant.

steps.title: Define the microscopic environment
steps.content:
To understand resistance, we look at the microscopic level of the wire. Electrical current is the flow of **charge carriers**, which are free electrons. As these electrons move through the wire, they do not have a clear path; instead, they constantly interact with the stationary **lattice ions** that make up the metal structure.

This interaction is described in **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**. Resistance essentially arises from these electrons "bumping" into the ions.

steps.title: Analyze increasing length
steps.content:
When the length $L$ of the wire is increased, the path that an electron must travel to get from one end to the other becomes longer. 

Because the electron has a longer distance to cover, it will undergo **more frequent collisions** with the lattice ions. Each collision acts as an obstacle, slowing down the drift velocity of the electrons and opposing the flow of current.

steps.title: Link collisions to resistance
steps.content:
Since resistance $R$ is a measure of the opposition to current flow, the increase in collisions directly results in an increase in resistance. 

Mathematically, this conceptual model supports the relationship shown in the data where resistance is directly proportional to length ($R \propto L$). For a uniform wire, doubling the length doubles the number of collisions, thereby doubling the resistance.

$$\text{Resistance} \propto \text{Number of Collisions} \propto \text{Length}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide R by L for the first two rows.

ti84.instructions: Divide the first resistance by its length, then do the same for the second point.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the ratio R/L for the first two values.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of Resistance to Length for the first two data points to verify the proportional relationship.

steps.title: Model segments in series
steps.content: To understand why resistance depends on length, imagine a long wire as being composed of several identical shorter segments of wire, each of length $\Delta L$, connected end-to-end.

steps.title: Apply series resistance rules
steps.content:
In physics, connecting components end-to-end is a **series combination**. As covered in **Topic B.5 (Current and circuits)**, the total resistance $R_{total}$ of resistors in series is the sum of their individual resistances:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \dots + R_n$$

If each small segment of wire has a resistance $r$, adding more segments to increase the length simply adds more resistance to the total.

steps.title: Relate length to total resistance
steps.content: Since the number of segments $n$ is directly proportional to the total length $L$, the total resistance $R$ increases linearly with length. If you double the length, you are effectively doubling the number of segments in series, which doubles the total resistance. This explains why $R$ is expected to be **directly proportional** to $L$.

steps.title: Confirm with resistivity formula
steps.content:
This physical model is summarized by the resistivity formula found in the IB Data Booklet:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ is the resistivity (constant for the material)
- $L$ is the length
- $A$ is the cross-sectional area

Because the question specifies a "constant wire of uniform cross-sectional area," both $\rho$ and $A$ are constant, confirming that $R \propto L$.

highlights.text: constant wire of uniform cross-sectional area

steps.title: Apply physical constraints
steps.content:
In **Topic B.5: Current and circuits**, we learn that the resistance $R$ of a uniform wire is given by:

$$R = \rho \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ is resistivity and $A$ is the cross-sectional area. Since the wire is uniform and constant, $R$ is directly proportional to $L$ ($R \propto L$). 

This physical principle tells us that if the length is zero ($L = 0$), the resistance must also be zero ($R = 0$). Therefore, our line of best fit **must pass through the origin $(0,0)$** as a theoretical data point.

1. Starts at the origin $(0,0)$.
2. Passes as close as possible to all data points, balancing those slightly above and below the line.

Looking at the data, the points follow a very consistent gradient of approximately $0.49$ $\Omega \text{ m}^{-1}$.
